source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Up to the time of the Olympiads there is no certain history among the Greeks, all things before that date being confused, and in no way consistent with each other.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: But these Olympiads were thoroughly investigated by many, as the Greeks made up the records of their history not according to long spaces, but in periods of four years.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For which reason I shall select the most remarkable of the mythical narratives before the time of the first Olympiad, and rapidly run over them.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: But those after that period, at least those that are notable, I shall take together, Hebrew events in connection with Greek, according to their dates, examining carefully the affairs of the Hebrews, and touching more cursorily on those of the Greeks; and my plan will be as follows: Taking up some single event in Hebrew history synchronous with another in Greek history, and keeping by it as the main subject, subtracting or adding as may seem needful in the narrative, I shall note what Greek or Persian of note, or remarkable personage of any other nationality, flourished at the date of that event in Hebrew history; and thus I may perhaps attain the object which I propose to myself.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: The most famous exile that befell the Hebrews, then—to wit, when they were led captive by Nabuchodonosor king of Babylon—lasted 70 years, as Jeremias had prophesied.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Berosus the Babylonian, moreover, makes mention of Nabuchodonosor.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And after the 70 years of captivity, Cyrus became king of the Persians at the time of the 55th Olympiad, as may be ascertained from the Bibliothecæ of Diodorus and the histories of Thallus and Castor, and also from Polybius and Phlegon, and others besides these, who have made the Olympiads a subject of study.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For the date is a matter of agreement among them all.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And Cyrus then, in the first year of his reign, which was the first year of the 55th Olympiad, effected the first partial restoration of the people by the hand of Zorobabel, with whom also was Jesus the son of Josedec, since the period of 70 years was now fulfilled, as is narrated in Esdra the Hebrew historian.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: The narratives of the beginning of the sovereignty of Cyrus and the end of the captivity accordingly coincide.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And thus, according to the reckoning of the Olympiads, there will be found a like harmony of events even to our time.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And by following this, we shall also make the other narratives fit in with each other in the same manner.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: But if the Attic time-reckoning is taken as the standard for affairs prior to these, then from Ogygus, who was believed by them to be an autochthon, in whose time also the first great flood took place in Attica, while Phoroneus reigned over the Argives, as Acusilaus relates, up to the date of the first Olympiad, from which period the Greeks thought they could fix dates accurately, there are altogether 1020 years; which number both coincides with the above-mentioned, and will be established by what follows.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For these things are also recorded by the Athenian historians Hellanicus and Philochorus, who record Attic affairs; and by Castor and Thallus, who record Syrian affairs; and by Diodorus, who writes a universal history in his Bibliothecæ; and by Alexander Polyhistor, and by some of our own time, yet more carefully, and by all the Attic writers.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Whatever narrative of note, therefore, meets us in these 1020 years, shall be given in its proper place.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: In accordance with this writing, therefore, we affirm that Ogygus, who gave his name to the first flood, and was saved when many perished, lived at the time of the exodus of the people from Egypt along with Moses.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And this we make out in the following manner.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: From Ogygus up to the first Olympiad already mentioned, it will be shown that there are 1020 years; and from the first Olympiad to the first year of the 55th, that is the first year of King Cyrus, which was also the end of the captivity, are 217 years.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: From Ogygus, therefore, to Cyrus are 1237.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And if one carries the calculation backwards from the end of the captivity, there are 1237 years.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Thus, by analysis, the same period is found to the first year of the exodus of Israel under Moses from Egypt, as from the 55th Olympiad to Ogygus, who founded Eleusis.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And from this point we get a more notable beginning for Attic chronography.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: So much, then, for the period prior to Ogygus.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And at his time Moses left Egypt.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And we demonstrate in the following manner how reliable is the statement that this happened at that date.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: From the exodus of Moses up to Cyrus, who reigned after the captivity, are 1237 years.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For the remaining years of Moses are 40.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: The years of Jesus, who led the people after him, are 25; those of the elders, who were judges after Jesus, are 30; those of the judges, whose history is given in the book of Judges, are 490; those of the priests Eli and Samuel are 90; those of the successive kings of the Hebrews are 490.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Then come the 70 years of the captivity, the last year of which was the first year of the reign of Cyrus, as we have already said.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And from Moses, then, to the first Olympiad there are 1020 years, as to the first year of the 55th Olympiad from the same are 1237, in which enumeration the reckoning of the Greeks coincides with us.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And after Ogygus, by reason of the vast destruction caused by the flood, the present land of Attica remained without a king up to Cecrops, a period of 189 years.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For Philochorus asserts that the Actæus who is said to have succeeded Ogygus, or whatever other fictitious names are adduced, never existed.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And again: From Ogygus, therefore, to Cyrus, says he, the same period is reckoned as from Moses to the same date, viz., 1237 years; and some of the Greeks also record that Moses lived at that same time.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Polemo, for instance, in the first book of his Greek History, says: In the time of Apis, son of Phoroneus, a division of the army of the Egyptians left Egypt, and settled in the Palestine called Syrian, not far from Arabia: these are evidently those who were with Moses.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And Apion the son of Poseidonius, the most laborious of grammarians, in his book Against the Jews, and in the fourth book of his History, says that in the time of Inachus king of Argos, when Amosis reigned over Egypt, the Jews revolted under the leadership of Moses.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And Herodotus also makes mention of this revolt, and of Amosis, in his second book, and in a certain way also of the Jews themselves, reckoning them among the circumcised, and calling them the Assyrians of Palestine, perhaps through Abraham.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And Ptolemy the Mendesian, who narrates the history of the Egyptians from the earliest times, gives the same account of all these things; so that among them in general there is no difference worth notice in the chronology.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: It should be observed, further, that all the legendary accounts which are deemed specially remarkable by the Greeks by reason of their antiquity, are found to belong to a period posterior to Moses; such as their floods and conflagrations, Prometheus, Io, Europa, the Sparti, the abduction of Proserpine, their mysteries, their legislations, the deeds of Dionysus, Perseus, the Argonauts, the Centaurs, the Minotaur, the affairs of Troy, the labours of Hercules, the return of the Heraclidæ, the Ionian migration and the Olympiads.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And it seemed good to me to give an account especially of the before-noted period of the Attic sovereignty, as I intend to narrate the history of the Greeks side by side with that of the Hebrews.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For any one will be able, if he only start from my position, to make out the reckoning equally well with me.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: Now, in the first year of that period of 1020 years, stretching from Moses and Ogygus to the first Olympiad, the passover and the exodus of the Hebrews from Egypt took place, and also in Attica the flood of Ogygus.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: And that is according to reason.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: For when the Egyptians were being smitten in the anger of God with hail and storms, it was only to be expected that certain parts of the earth should suffer with them; and, in especial, it was but to be expected that the Athenians should participate in such calamity with the Egyptians, since they were supposed to be a colony from them, as Theopompus alleges in his Tricarenus, and others besides him.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: The intervening period has been passed by, as no remarkable event is recorded during it among the Greeks.

verses.book: The Extant Fragments of the Five Books of the Chronography of Julius Africanus.
verses.text: But after 94 years Prometheus arose, according to some, who was fabulously reported to have formed men; for being a wise man, he transformed them from the state of extreme rudeness to culture.
